content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 215–216 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_018_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_018_page_002.jpg` | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `DEU_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Priests and Levites — Their Portion Is the LORD (vv. 1–8)

**1** The Levitical priests — the whole tribe of Levi — shall have no allotment or inheritance with Israel. They shall live on the food offerings made to the LORD by fire, which are their inheritance. **2** They shall have no inheritance among their fellow Israelites — the LORD himself is their inheritance, just as he promised them.

**3** This is the priests' due from the people: whoever slaughters an ox or a sheep must give the priest the shoulder, the two cheeks, and the stomach. **4** You shall also give the priests the firstfruits of your grain, new wine, and olive oil, and the first shearing of your sheep. **5** For the LORD your God has chosen them out of all your tribes to stand and minister in the LORD's name — them and their descendants for all generations.

**6** If a Levite comes from any town anywhere in Israel — wherever he has been living — and comes in earnest desire to the place the LORD will choose, **7** he may minister in the name of the LORD his God like all his fellow Levites who stand before the LORD there. **8** He shall receive an equal share of the food to eat, even if he has additional income from family property.

---

### No Divination — The Nations' Detestable Practices (vv. 9–14)

**9** When you enter the land the LORD your God is giving you, do not learn to imitate the detestable practices of the nations there. **10** Let no one be found among you who sacrifices their son or daughter in the fire, who practices divination or reads omens, interprets signs, practices sorcery, **11** casts spells, consults a medium or a spiritist, or inquires of the dead. **12** Anyone who does these things is detestable to the LORD, and because of these detestable practices the LORD your God is driving those nations out before you. **13** You must be blameless before the LORD your God. **14** The nations you are dispossessing listen to those who read omens and practice divination — but the LORD your God has not permitted you to do that.

---

### The Prophet Like Moses — Listen to Him (vv. 15–22)

**15** The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you — from your own people. Listen to him. **16** This is exactly what you asked for at Horeb on the day of the assembly, when you said, "Do not let us hear the voice of the LORD our God or see this great fire anymore, or we will die."

**17** The LORD said to me: "What they have said is good. **18** I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their own people — I will put my words in his mouth, and he will speak to them everything I command him. **19** I myself will call to account anyone who does not listen to my words that the prophet speaks in my name. **20** But a prophet who dares to speak in my name anything I did not command, or who speaks in the name of other gods — that prophet shall die."

**21** You may be wondering, "How can we know when a message has not come from the LORD?" **22** When a prophet speaks in the name of the LORD and what they say does not come true or does not happen — that is a message the LORD did not speak. That prophet has spoken presumptuously. Have no fear of them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 2 — "the LORD himself is their inheritance"**: Hebrew *YHWH hu nachalato* — the LORD, he is his inheritance. This sentence is one of the most theologically dense in Deuteronomy. Every other tribe receives land — tangible, agricultural, wealth-generating property. The Levites receive God. Their material provision comes through the people's offerings, but their identity rests on a different foundation entirely: proximity to God is their portion.

**vv. 3–4 — Priestly portions**: The shoulder, cheeks, and stomach of every animal slaughter — plus grain, wine, oil, and wool — constitute the Levitical support system. This is structural economics, not charity: the community's worship automatically sustains those who lead worship. PNEUMA notes that this provision is not merely pragmatic but theological — you cannot separate the priest from the offering; both belong to the same sacred economy.

**v. 6 — "comes in earnest desire"**: Hebrew *b'chol awat nafsho* — with all the longing/desire of his soul. Dynamic: "comes in earnest desire." The Levite who travels from a rural town to the central sanctuary to minister is motivated by genuine spiritual yearning, not career opportunity. That desire is to be honored with equal access and equal provision.

**vv. 9–14 — The forbidden arts**: PNEUMA renders this list as a catalogue of ways ancient peoples tried to access divine knowledge outside of God's appointed means. Child sacrifice (v. 10) sits first in the list — the most extreme form of seeking supernatural power through human destruction. Divination, omen-reading, sorcery, casting spells, mediumship, necromancy — all are rejected not primarily because they are "superstitious nonsense" (some evidently worked; see 1 Sam 28) but because they represent unauthorized, relationship-bypassing access to the divine realm.

**v. 13 — "blameless"**: Hebrew *tamim* — whole, complete, without defect. The same word used for unblemished sacrificial animals. Israel is called to be an offering of wholeness before God — not fragmented by divided loyalties to other spiritual powers.

**v. 14 — "The LORD your God has not permitted you to do that"**: The contrast is stark. The nations listen to diviners because they have no other access to divine guidance. Israel has the LORD speaking through prophets. Divination is not merely forbidden — it is unnecessary, a sign of orphaned religion seeking contact with heaven without a Father.

**v. 15 — "A prophet like me"**: This is the central messianic text of Deuteronomy. Moses does not describe a king, a priest, or a warrior — but a prophet: someone who speaks God's words to the people in God's name. The New Testament applies this explicitly to Jesus (Acts 3:22; 7:37; John 6:14; 1:21). PNEUMA renders the verse with its full weight: "Listen to him" is the command — identical in logic to the command to listen to Moses.

**vv. 16–17 — The request at Horeb**: The people's terrified request at Sinai/Horeb — "do not let us hear God's voice directly" — becomes the theological rationale for prophecy. God accommodates human limitations by speaking through human intermediaries. The prophetic office is born from mercy as much as from divine initiative.

**v. 18 — "I will put my words in his mouth"**: Hebrew *v'natati d'varai b'fiv* — the prophet does not generate divine words; he receives and transmits them. This is verbal inspiration in its most direct form — not mechanical dictation but a living word planted in a human speaker.

**v. 20 — "That prophet shall die"**: The false prophet who claims to speak for YHWH while speaking from himself, or who ventriloquizes for other gods, faces the same penalty as the idolater and the town that worships other gods. False prophecy is not merely wrong theology — it is treason against the covenant relationship between God and Israel.

**vv. 21–22 — The test of prophecy**: The practical test — does it come true? — applies to conditional predictions. PNEUMA notes this is a necessary but not sufficient criterion (see ch. 13: even fulfilled signs do not validate a prophet who leads away from YHWH). The temporal test catches false prophets whose words fail history; the theological test of ch. 13 catches those whose words succeed but corrupt. Both tests together form a comprehensive framework. — *PNEUMA*
